### Lyndal — 2018-03-02T00:42:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-sex-is-bad.20780/post-236104>

Every body is unique, every sexual experience is unique. When it comes to sex, there is not a one size fits all approach.

Sure there’s general ideas, rules, approaches, techniques, styles and so forth. You however, have to work out what fits you best.

Sex is a journey, not a destination and sex, orgasm especially, doesn’t just happen in the vagina or the penis!

Personal power, self responsibility, knowing ones own body - likes and dislikes - pain and pleasure, breath, communication, consent, mind power (for want of a better description), freedom to experiment, making mistakes, education, are key to how you can experience great sex. Let alone basic mental, physical, spiritual, emotional awareness.

If you don’t know what works for you, how can you expect anybody else to? If you don’t trust yourself or your own body, how can you expect anybody else to? If you don’t know NO is a complete sentence, how can you expect anybody else to? If you don’t know your own boundaries, how can you expect anybody else to? Etc.,.

Do you know you can orgasm without ejaculating?
Do you know women can ejaculate?
Do you know you can experience orgasm without even being touched, touching yourself?
Do you know orgasm can last for as long as you’d like it to last? Hours?
Do you know it’s ok to have sex purley for the pleasure of it. For the fabulous health benefits too?

You probably do know all this. Just in case you don’t.

Slut shaming is rife. Sex shaming in general is rife. Judgments are rife. Assumptions are rife. Moral high grounds are rife. Fear...fear...fear is rife.

Just because I wouldn’t do a particular act you like doing or I choose to do a particular act, that you wouldn’t or don’t like doing, doesn’t make it wrong.

Nudity is still such a taboo. Is it any wonder sex is a greater taboo?

Thank you Dr J. K., these kinds of open dialogues are so important. I’d love to live in a world where we have discussions about sex, like we do our sports!

There is some great porn happening out there now too. Real sex, not the Hollywood style.

As for sex workers, they don’t need to be rescued. They are not broken. It’s a valid career path. If you think about it, if you’re in any kind of job, you’re selling your body, what’s the difference?

As you can tell, this is an area I’m incredibly passionate about. I don’t claim to be any expert. I’ve been fortunate to have had my own sexual revolution ignited. I trust there is something of benefit in my sharing.

### Earth Monkey — 2018-03-08T00:51:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-sex-is-bad.20780/post-236646>

The only times in my life that intercourse was painfull for me were when I had vulvodynia. It felt like the guy's penis was a knife and I didn't know what it was the first time, then it went away. The second time, 3 years ago (nearly 10 years later) and after 9 months of pain, I saw a doctor about it and she couldn't work out what it was and said maybe I'd just got too tight and if I got desperate there were operations to loosen things up!! So then I researched it myself, came up with vulvodynia and discovered the oxalate and mold connection. I was living in a moldy (various, including stachybotris) apartment at the time and eating a lot of very high oxalate foods common on a 'popular paleo diet'. So I went on a low oxalate diet and moved to an unmoldy house and within a few months the pain was noticeably less. At around the 6mth mark the pain was gone and now, more than 2.5 years after my self diagnosis, I'm still pain-free. I would recommend anyone experiencing this kind of pain to try a low oxalate diet, in addition to their usual mitohacking (vag sunning, etc). It shocks me that so many doctors don't seem to know anything about this, quite straightfoward, medical condition which I suspect is the cause of the pain of most of the 30% mentioned in the article experiencing pain. Just be sure to read up on the diet 1st, as you need to transition to it to prevent various 'oxalate dumping' symptoms. the Vulvar Pain foundation is one of the best resources, but there's a few FB groups etc too. [http://www.thevpfoundation.org/vpfoxalate.htm](http://www.thevpfoundation.org/vpfoxalate.htm)

The nature of the dopamine based addictions ( and porn addiction is a real thing) is that the more stimulus you get the more it takes to get that dopamine hit. There are young men in their teens and 20s nowadays having a soft time getting excited when they interact with a real live woman due to the availability of porn. There is a no-fap movement out there right now where men abstain from porn and masturbation to try to get their ability to achieve and maintain an erection back when in the presence of a real live sexual partner.

> When people say this kind of stuff they say it as if they are offering you gold bullion, as if it were something which would carry you through life and bring you some kind of benefit. It holds no benefit.
> They think they're in heaven but they're living in hell. It's no fun being a vampire. Another gent from masterbating too much couldn't make love to his wife, he had to sit in the corner all alone and well, not sure what his wife did, but I do know that they divorced. I ran out of NY when I did because well, it was very, twisted. So happy I ran for the hills. Not to say that we shouldn't have compassion for those who hit the wall and inadvertently became vampires.
>
> There is a book that I read some decades back, it's called 'Trauma in Combat'. Because some soldiers suffer from horrific events that keep a fixation in their mind they all too often try to counteract the horrors with sex.
> I have a friend who never saw combat. His appointment was to go in after battle and pick up the pieces; hands, feet, fingers, oesophagus' and heads and bag them up. He is deeply disturbed, so it's not only front-line problems. He had such a great life before going to war, and now he has this other personality that he has to fight with for the rest of his life.
> In some respects I think that is where the harem must have originated. Mass murder begets vampires, the only way to assuage the horror of what one witnessed in war was to drown yourself in sex. (Like they did in Jamaica during the endless war against Spain for their freedom against slavery. The men gave birth to 50 children because of the slaughter to repopulate. )
>

So the masters of deceit keep this intrigue going amidst the youth, because they make big money off it. In the music industry you can't get a contract if you don't have sex or violence in the lyrics. The biggest consumer of such tunes & lyrics are the young white upper class. My friend had to log into a computer the pieces of bodies from the 911 world trade towers, piece by piece. He was out of work for awhile and when this job came he had to take it. He's struggling too.

I try to tell the young women this because women go through heat and it's difficult; music and dancing help.

### caroline — 2018-03-13T00:38:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-sex-is-bad.20780/post-237084>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=237079)
>
> How, when did you knew that you are ready for another man?
> .

Hi Jan .....it was a quantum journey to be sure.

I found Jack on MDA a few months after my DH passed. I knew I had found "gold" Jack made so much sense and I was committed to take the journey with him, even tho I struggle with all the science. I knew I didn't need a PHD to understand the three legged stool!

I spent long days at the ocean, ate epi paleo. C/T'd my butt off. turned off all electronics, turned off the hot water in my apartment, and the circuit breaker.

Before long - I wasn't invisible any longer. Before long I was happy with myself - I weighed less than I had in years, I was sleeping well, and best of all I felt very happy and full of energy.

As Jack has said many times ....we need to love ourselves first before we can expect someone else too - absolute words of wisdom.

The biggest step was going to Orlando to meet Jack. He told me something that changed everything for me - just something very simple but it was gold for me.

I also had the privilege of meeting Stella in Orlando and we became best friends for life ...sisters from a different mother. Stella and I have continued on this optimal journey together. It has been life altering for both of us.

I felt, in Orlando, that I was at ground zero ........and amazing things started to happen and continue to happen.

So that is a long and round about way of saying .......once you can love and appreciate yourself then it leaves room for someone wonderful to add another dimension.

Once you become open to all possibilities - others gravitate towards you. It turns out that a lovely man was looking for someone to share his life with and he was willing to be patient.

I went from " I don't know if I can" to trusting hm completely in a very short period of time.

Two other important things .......Stella had to love him too and he was interested and a willing participant in all this! Now he says "Jack says" more than me!

I can plainly reach out and tell the man how glad that I've gotten to know him over the last year and that I look up to the amount of fortitude and perseverance that he practices in a tough time and that I hope I'd be able to pull off if or when I get in a situation that's similar.

Or instead throw in a breathy test half-joke (because the best ones are mostly truth) and throw in a picture of a stallion. In man-English it conveys pretty much the same thing.

Yep, the new profile pic looks great.

A man who can wait to make a decision until it's best for him is a man a woman can trust to lead. A man who hurries things is a man who is uncertain about his future options: he wants to spend his little power now in case he loses the chance.

Not looks, not height, not fitness, not intelligence, not wealth, not clothes, not humor.

Patience.

We have relative heaping boatloads of self-reflection, language, and abstraction amongst all the mammals. Even after raising your youngest offspring you have more life experience that you can share and youngsters that will ponder this. I don't think any older chimp is able to experience, process, and convey their reflective experience through language that younger chimps can immediately apply toward thinking about how they want to try structure their future life.

[http://www.wellbeingwithnutrition.c...rticles-all/9-articles/6-cellular-dehydration](http://www.wellbeingwithnutrition.co.uk/index.php/articles/articles-all/9-articles/6-cellular-dehydration)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-dehydration.20781/post-233789>

### Allin — 2018-02-10T01:33:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-dehydration.20781/post-234466>

> [Brando said:](/goto/post?id=233787)
>
> Is this info correct?
>
>
> [http://www.wellbeingwithnutrition.c...rticles-all/9-articles/6-cellular-dehydration](http://www.wellbeingwithnutrition.co.uk/index.php/articles/articles-all/9-articles/6-cellular-dehydration)

**"The Main Factors Contributing to Cellular Dehydration"**
Lifestyle stress which may be any combination of physical, emotional or mental stress triggers the fight and flight response which in turn triggers cellular dehydration. Other significant factors contributing to cellular dehydration include diet and nutritional imbalances.

I read the article it reminded me of all the "then current" information I read 20 years ago.....but I did not see any mention of sunlight, distance between Cytochrome 1 to 5, efficiency of ECT or major cause of dehydration by nnEMF and blue light toxicity or need of DHA.

### JanSz — 2018-02-10T01:51:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-dehydration.20781/post-234469>

One can be all swollen and dehydrated at the same time.

swollen------>too much extracellular water---->practically not much different than tap water ----incorrect electrolyte levels

dehydrated ------>not enough water is made in mitochondria (usually because there is too much D+ instead of H+ there).
water made by mitochondria in matrix area is/should be made of H+
D+ is too large to fit thru ATPase. Breaks it when trying ----> heteroplasmy
......................

To correct eat low D food, drink low D water.

Work on supporting body processes that remove D.
Support smaller mitochondria.

////////

Hello guys, hope you all doing well with optimizing your lifes! Am looking for thoughts how to deal with scars left on face due to teen-age years. Also stretch marks caused by being fat and then losing weight. I heard once about DHEA cream for stretch marks, didn't confirm tho. Now about scars maybe cold?? I would really appreciate it guys..

[https://www.jackkruse.com/why-the-epi-paleo-rx-is-stat-paging-your-brain/](https://www.jackkruse.com/why-the-epi-paleo-rx-is-stat-paging-your-brain/)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-30T18:27:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-233819>

September 10th, 2011 at 4:32 pm at MDA
@Judy in response to your email about **loose skin and stretch marks and how to deal with them.** I responded to this in the MDA monster thread around page 83 on my iPad. Here is the response. Some one asked about loose skin post weight loss too……here are my thoughts.
after my patients lose their weight we use a few things to tighten skin over about 12 months…..high dose Vit K2 and teprenone (Oral and topical), caprylic acid (oral and topical) and high dose dietary palm and [coconut oil](https://hcgdietinfo.com/hcgdietforums/redirect-to/?redirect=http%3A%2F%2Fthrv.me%2F2r). It does amazing things to adipocytes and the skin once the weight is gone. CT also helps a ton.

Some people will also add a couple of high dose supplements too….NAC and R-lipoic acid and resveratrol. But this can cause a proton spin issue. This is not for a leptin reset…….this is when you are looking to lose the last twenty pounds after your weight loss.

If there is a lot of **cellulite marks** I also ask patients to consider high dose super curcumin as well.

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-08-02T19:31:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-247313>

Systemic enzymes taken orally have been touted as an effective remedy for scar tissue removal. They work by dissolving scar tissue through enzymatic action.

They are reputed to dissolve scars both cosmetically (externally) and internally (fibrosis conditions anywhere in body, as in kidneys for example).

I had some skin scars that I tried proteolytic enzyme therapy (fairly high dose- must be taken on empty stomach so that it does not get used up in digesting food). I have done this for several years.

So far, perhaps a slight reduction in scar extent and visibility, but no magical removal powers.

Perhaps this approach would be more effective with freshly made scars that are still amenable to scar tissue remodeling process by body.

Also, where **fibrin** is involved vs. **collagen** connective tissue that got scrambled, special fibrolytic enzymes like **nattokinase** and **serrapeptase** may be more effective.

Some people use certain, specific essential oils for scar treatment, but not me so far.

Anyway, that is my foot soldier boots on ground experience here.

>

I've had similar lackluster results with proteolytic and "systemic" enzymes starting in the early 90's.

I lacked perspective on what enzymes did at a quantum level. Without the right environment and conditions I think it's like trying to run a car on just fuel line cleaner without any actual fuel.

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-04-05T02:22:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-267358>

Gotu Kola and proteolytic enzymes, together with collagen, hyaluronic acid and other interventions might be the ticket to beat scar tissue. But again, time is of the essence to begin treatment as soon as possible after the scarring event to help ensure some success. Once scar tissue remodeling is done by the body I believe it is extremely hard to undo (that is, dissolve scars into normal, flexible tissue).

I think this natural treatment approach would be a real aid in treating Peyronie's Disease in men. PD is a deformity in the penis caused by scar tissue from injuries (it can result in pain during intercourse and abnormal curvature of the member among other things). It is not a trivial disorder because it can cause a lot of suffering in some men, and is probably more widespread than officially reported.

PD sufferers needs effective non-surgical treatments so this brings a bit of hope to the situation.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-267778>

Natto stinks to high heaven, but apparently it does wonders to reduce wrinkles in women.

Asian women, both in their native countries and those living in USA are known for having fewer wrinkles than white women as they age-- this is attributed to K2 vitamin from natto (but aging skin is multi factorial so don't rule out other lifestyle habits as contributing factors as well-- women who smoked during their lives are noticeably wrinkled as a general rule).

Fair complected women with North European ancestry seem to fare worst in the skin aging game. I think this is because their facial skin is more thin and delicate. So sad.

K2 is available in supplements in case your nose wrinkles at smell of natto (pun intended), including those derived from natural sources instead of synthetic forms.

I'm genuinely curious by your "so sad" comment - do you think Caucasian men's skin ages better than women's?

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-04-13T18:37:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-268000>

> [Novah said:](/goto/post?id=267957)
>
> I'm genuinely curious by your "so sad" comment - do you think Caucasian men's skin ages better than women's?

Men's attractiveness is held to a different standard than women generally. An older male with some rugged aging lines and distinguished gray hair is often seen as masculine, alpha, and desirable. Wrinkles, saggy skin, and gray hair on aging women is viewed with horror by many women (but other ladies can and will age with dignity and don't try to keep up appearances with tons of cosmetics).

That is why it can be sad for white women who don't age all that well. It may indeed be shallow (beauty can be only skin deep for some) but the female of species counts on physical attractiveness as a major asset. When eye candy-ness hits the wall with aging it can be distressing for some women who mainly relied on physical beauty. So sad.

Although now politically incorrect to mention, like it or not, white women are indeed the gold standard of female beauty (and no other ethnic group has such a wonderful, wide variety of hair, eye, and skin colors as Caucasian women). Women of color in other societies are often judged more desirable with higher social status the paler their skin is. Not my opinion-- that is just the way the world works.

So ladies treat your skin well while you are young and it will be healthy and attractive for much of your later lives.

### Novah — 2019-04-14T01:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-268022>

> [ElectricUniverse said:](/goto/post?id=268000)
>
> Men's attractiveness is held to a different standard than women generally. An older male with some rugged aging lines and distinguished gray hair is often seen as masculine, alpha, and desirable. Wrinkles, saggy skin, and gray hair on aging women is viewed with horror by many women (but other ladies can and will age with dignity and don't try to keep up appearances with tons of cosmetics).
>
> That is why it can be sad for white women who don't age all that well. It may indeed be shallow (beauty can be only skin deep for some) but the female of species counts on physical attractiveness as a major asset. When eye candy-ness hits the wall with aging it can be distressing for some women who mainly relied on physical beauty. So sad.

Thanks for your honest opinion. I may not agree with it but appreciate it regardless.

I say this with respect, but it seems like you’re an older fella and with that comes beliefs of that generation.

Things have changed...a lot.

As women have worked, gained education, income and independence, that double standard of beauty has been shifting, especially for anyone under 50. And the media has had a massive influence in shifting perceptions as well.

What we’re seeing nowadays is men being held to a higher standard of beauty than ever before.

As for society allowing men to age easier than women, I think that’s debatable, especially nowadays.

Here’s a few snippets of info… I'll put my final thoughts at the end.

\*\*\*\*\*​

“Men are facing mounting pressure to sculpt perfect physiques and spend a fortune on grooming and clothes.

Can you imagine Cary Grant tinting his eyebrows? Or a young Sean Connery on the Paleo diet? Would Steve McQueen have agonised over his eye creams and anxiously measured his body fat index?

Probably not, but the timeless icons of male beauty and style are increasingly irrelevant to today’s modern men. The reflection staring back at us now is entirely different to the one that met our forefathers, because now more than ever men are in the throes of an aesthetic crisis. The notions of what is considered handsome and what is considered ‘masculine’ have shifted dramatically.

Grappling with society’s critical gaze and unrealistic body images has been a thorn in women’s sides for generations, so perhaps it was only natural that it would gradually permeate the male psyche.” [https://www.telegraph.co.uk/men/fas...-been-so-preoccupied-by-their-appearance.html](https://www.telegraph.co.uk/men/fashion-and-style/10814392/Men-have-never-been-so-preoccupied-by-their-appearance.html)

\*\*\*\*\*​

“Why should we care about male body image? A massive “95% of college age men are dissatisfied with their bodies on some level” (Daniel & Bridges 2013). That’s a huge portion of each campus’s population! We need to pay more attention to male body image and the struggles men may face when coming to terms with their imperfect bodies. Just as today’s society expects women to become thinner with statuesque features, American men, and men all across Western cultures, feel pressure to pump up their bodies and slim down, creating a combination of lean, bulky muscle.

However, unhealthy male body image extends beyond the average college man. According to a study, over 90% of men struggle in some way with body dissatisfaction and negative affect (negative opinions of self), or negative emotions and thoughts towards one’s body (Castonguay et al. 2014). Research also shows that body image disorders may be more severe in both gay and heterosexual teenage-through-young-adult (post-college) males than men in other age categories (Burlew & Shurts 2013).

As a culture we know very little about the prevalence of this issue in men and boys since body image is traditionally considered a ‘female problem’. The fact is, men can suffer just as much body dissatisfaction as women, but we may pay less attention to male body image because men are quieter about these problems: Men tend to seek treatment, counseling, or positive solutions less frequently, or they hold off on doing so out of shame and embarrassment (Burlew & Shurts 2013). [https://www.bradley.edu/sites/bodyproject/male-body-image-m-vs-f/](https://www.bradley.edu/sites/bodyproject/male-body-image-m-vs-f/)

\*\*\*\*\*​

“It has become increasingly prevalent in entertainment, media, and [advertising](https://go.redirectingat.com/?id=74679X1524629&sref=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.buzzfeed.com%2Feugeneyang%2Fmens-standards-of-beauty-around-the-world&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.adweek.com%2Fnews%2Fadvertising-branding%2Fhunkvertising-objectification-men-advertising-152925&xcust=3727134%7CAMP&xs=1) in recent years to focus on the beauty of the "ideal" male form. Although studies have shown that women continue to report higher numbers in regards to negative body image, the percentage of men admitting to body dissatisfaction is, shockingly, growing at a much faster rate.” [https://www.buzzfeed.com/eugeneyang/mens-standards-of-beauty-around-the-world](https://www.buzzfeed.com/eugeneyang/mens-standards-of-beauty-around-the-world)

\*\*\*\*\*​

“Male body image and its associated disorders have become a silent epidemic that is paralysing men. It’s a matter of quality of life, and life and death.... And consider these statistics:

[25 percent](https://thebutterflyfoundation.org.au/understand-eating-disorders/eating-disorder-myths/) of all people with anorexia nervosa or bulimia nervosa are male.

[40 percent](https://thebutterflyfoundation.org.au/understand-eating-disorders/eating-disorder-myths/) of people with a binge eating disorder are male.

[42 percent](https://www.nationaleatingdisorders.org/eating-disorders-lgbt-populations) of males who have eating disorders identify as gay.

[25 percent](https://www.betterhealth.vic.gov.au/health/healthyliving/body-image-men#lp-h-2) of Australian men in a healthy weight range believe they’re fat.

[3 percent](https://www.betterhealth.vic.gov.au/health/healthyliving/body-image-men#lp-h-2) of Australian teenage boys use muscle-enhancing drugs like steroids.

Keep in mind the stigma surrounding these issues for men means cases are under-reported.

There’s still the misconception among men that body image issues and eating disorders only affect women.” [https://www.sbs.com.au/guide/article/2017/06/13/comment-we-need-talk-about-male-body-image](https://www.sbs.com.au/guide/article/2017/06/13/comment-we-need-talk-about-male-body-image)

\*\*\*\*\*​

“Body image concerns more men than women, research finds. -- More men worry about their body shape and appearance – beer bellies, "man boobs" or going bald – than women do about how they look, according to research. More than four in five men (80.7%) talk in ways that promote anxiety about their body image by referring to perceived flaws and imperfections, compared with 75% of women. Similarly, 38% of men would sacrifice at least a year of their life in exchange for a perfect body – again, a higher proportion than women.” [https://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2012/jan/06/body-image-concerns-men-more-than-women](https://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2012/jan/06/body-image-concerns-men-more-than-women)

\*\*\*\*\*​

Anyone, male or female, who focuses solely on looks is going to struggle as they age.

I think at the end of the day, no matter what you look like or how old you are, if you do your best to keep yourself healthy, happy and social, with a twinkle in your eye, a bit of wit and a smile on your face, and a genuine appreciation for others, you’ll likely always get attention — and if you feel you need a little touch up, and that makes you happy, knock yourself out — the happy couples I know that have been together for 40+ years both still think the other looks good which in my books is incredibly sweet.

At the end of the day, I think we need to try to be kind to each other and promote positive body image in both men and women rather than saying things like “white women’s skin doesn’t age well, so sad” —because that attitude also works in reverse these days towards men (including yourself) as well — instead, look for beauty and uniqueness in people — maybe they have great eyes or a great smile or a great figure etc. Or a wicked sense of humor that makes you laugh so hard you temporarily forget all your worries and love being around them. There’s so much beauty in the world, it’s simply a matter of seeing it.

Anyways, we’re way off topic for what this forum is meant for, but I thought it important to address. Take care.

Well, I never said physical beauty was the end-all and be-all of human condition. Far from it.

Yes, it seems true that some men have joined women these days in becoming more vain in physical appearance. I chalk this up simply to current dodgy societal trends, marketing, the media, etc. rather than any lasting existential truths.

By the way, there's nothing wrong with a person wanting to present their best _natural_ appearance to the world. It is something we should expect of each other.

Perhaps the overemphasis on the ephemeral (physical appearance) is a symptom of people living lives void of true meaning.

Also, today both men and women are afraid of perceived social consequences of appearing old.

Today's cultural environment is wacky and bent out of shape anyway with people deciding they want to be another gender or race than they were born with.

And a final comment on men's confusion about their relationships with women-- a growing trend seems to be MGTOW movement to avoid dealing with modern women (and no, I am not one of them!).

from Novah's above post ....how insightful and lovely. This is totally on point \[in my humble opinion\] of what is taking place at jackkruse.com.

Jack has been giving us all the science ...... and now Jack has resurrected himself to include the nitty gritty human/heart/soul side to all of us.

I just think it is wonderful .......there is so much more to our life that needs attention. Noticing someone's beautiful smile or wicked sense of humour or their uniqueness.

We are all so unique ....we all have so much to offer. It is so fantastic to really "see" the people in our lives and we will reap huge rewards.

I think all of this is what gives us passion, and puts a huge, silly smile on our face!

A few years ago this forum was oozing with this kind of stuff. If you have time ...have a look at some old threads - Jack started one - "say something about the one you love" and another one I started about women in dresses. Also oxy, oxy, oxy .......there is a lot to be said for swanning around in tight shorts!

My DH - to me - is the most handsome guy in the world. His love for me, his honour, his kindness to everyone - not just me, his smile when he sees me, his wonderful laugh and twinkle in his eye, his unswerving devotion to me. He is completely all in.
If you were to meet my guy - you would think he is a little short ass pommey guy - and he is! He is absolutely perfect ....for me!

### Inger — 2019-05-07T10:05:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-269845>

Yes. What is wrong with wrinkles? They give you character! I have tons...lol because I was always sun addicted... But I do not care. Sure, I use the redlight now to maybe improve my skin, but I am just fine with my wrinkles too. If they are to stay..

Emilia, the 6 yo little girl of my landlord, told me one day as she asked what green tea stuff I had on my kitchen counter was about, and I replied: I use it to maybe get less wrinkles
she then looked at me and said;
But Inger, if your wrinkles go away you are no more pretty!

I will never forget that. It was the honesty of a 6 yo. And she is very honest, always.
Made me think. Maybe if we are laughing a lot and our wrinkles look rather happy, it is a plus to have wrinkles versus not wrinkles  ? Sure, to have worry-wrinkles and angry wrinkles might not be so pretty...lol So let us instead make our lives happy to get happy wrinkles and stop thinking wrinkles are bad

That said,
my redlight has helped the scar from last summer fade quite a bit  It was not too pretty because i had the wound a ton in the sun. I wanted it to heal and did not care how the scar will look. Needless to say. it made the scar more noticeable... But the redlight has helped that

Inger, so glad you are comfortable in your own skin. Developing wrinkles and sagging, lackluster skin as we grow old are honorable war badges in the game of life. Be thankful you have lived to an age where wrinkles appear.

That said, there is also nothing wrong with improving our aging skin to look more attractive to others. Everyone is pleased with a face that is youthful, has a healthy glow, and exudes internal health. That is a service we can give ourselves and our fellow travelers.

From what I have seen with myself and others, using hydrolyzed collagen protein supplementation is probably the _single_ best practice you can do to get glowing, smooth skin back. And detox vigorously to get the toxins out. There is plenty more you can do.

You may want to get a copy of book _**Glow 15**_ by Naomi Whittel. She has some wonderful ideas for maintaining healthy skin as we age, especially for the ladies.

> From what I have seen with myself and others, using **hydrolyzed collagen protein supplementation** is probably the _single_ best practice you can do to get glowing, smooth skin back. And detox vigorously to get the toxins out. There is plenty more you can do.
>
> You may want to get a copy of book _**Glow 15**_ by Naomi Whittel. She has some wonderful ideas for maintaining healthy skin as we age, especially for the ladies.

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-05-08T18:06:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-fade-scars-stretch-marks.20782/post-269947>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=269891)
>
> **hydrolyzed collagen protein supplementation**
>
> **To me that looks like good quality bone broth in [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) speak?**
>
> **///////////**

Of course bone broth is a rich source of collagen, and a very healthy food overall (if you can find a clean source of it).

Collagen products on the market are a dicey proposition, since I have heard that they are sourced in dodgy areas in Asia where you have no control over cleanliness or contamination.

I prefer marine-based collagen-- it might be more pure and is less 'gamey' in taste (musky animal food taste and smell nauseate me).

Remember that collagen is not the kind of protein that is going to build muscle mass in particular. Collagen is for the skin, hair, nails, ligaments, fascia, etc. In other words, mainly for the **integumentary system.**

I realizing now I need to act sooner with my actions. It is a hundred times better then 3 years ago but its lingering now. If you want to know what if feels like when that area is f'd up. Its like your body is has a short circuit. Can' keep your eyes open because they hurt by light and flashing lights. Heart races then slow down. Hot then cold. So on. Today since I found this site I made major improvements but before this site I used progesterone to heal me to some degree but something else was missing and my instincts to keep looking for the cure. So I need to heal this Ammon horn before it becomes too damaged. I totally feel it is a inflammation. FYI, it comes and goes but some days bad some I don't feel it. ### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-30T18:22:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammons-horn.20784/post-233816>

Tinnitus is a warning shot in our children from their cochlea/endolymphatic that your redox level in Ammon's horn is not good at all. Sound waves (phonons) are the pulse of the ocean in our cells, in our cochlea, or endolymphic sac and gives the spark of life to our ears that only nature is capable of giving children. Hearing is a sense built around the air in our atmosphere immediately around us. The atmopshere is a sphere within a sphere and it can create and electrical tension within the magnetosphere and the surface of earth where the gases we breathe and exhale is located. The Earth is a sphere surrounded by the sphere of ionosphere. That tension becomes an "acoustic pulse wave" for things that live within both spheres. When the tension changes in the environment the pattern of signaling in the hearing mechanisms changes things in these sensory cells. Blue light and nnEMF change the tension in that sphere to increase ubiquitination rates on all things.

### taiyang — 2018-04-21T16:23:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/billy-bats-susan-labs.20785/post-239641>

Hi Billy. non- doctor here. your c-reactive protein was high. were you fighting a cold/infection at the time of the blood draw? i once was fighting an infection when i had a blood draw. it is counter-productive to test c-reactive protein when you are sick. mine was 10. i tested a month later after i was't sick anymore and it was back to normal. a low carb diet/high protein can dehydrate you- that is my comment on the BUN/creatinine ratio. ALT/AST is liver function. one of yours was high. mine is usually great. it only went up the year i religiously took vita d and iron supplements. unless you know for sure a supplement is helping you, i would not take ANY. that action may improve your liver function.

# Thread: Juicing Veggies: One step forward but two steps back?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing-veggies-one-step-forward-but-two-steps-back.20789/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Greenie — 2018-01-31T01:23:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing-veggies-one-step-forward-but-two-steps-back.20789/post-233842>

Having given a lot of thought to magnetic fields recently, I decided to grab a milligauss meter and see what my juicer produced while in operation. I couldn't believe how strong a field the juicer produced. The meter read .14m/gs before and shot up as far as 80m/gs; it vacillated between 65-80m/gs. (To put this in perspective: standing under the closest electrical power lines measures 25m/gs.)

With a magnetic field that strong, I began to wonder if the strength of these nnEMFs negated the nutritional content of the juice, much like heating certain oils that convert them from beneficial to deleterious.

Dr. Gerald Pollack advocated drinking vegetable juice in a recent interview due to the high EZ contained in vegetables. I wonder if and how EZ gets affected by nnEMF of such magnitude.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-31T02:51:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing-veggies-one-step-forward-but-two-steps-back.20789/post-233850>

fruits and juices have more deuterium and deuterium has an abnormally large magnetic moment..........a blender radically effects the juice. I think Pollack is dead wrong on this

### Greenie — 2018-01-31T03:05:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/juicing-veggies-one-step-forward-but-two-steps-back.20789/post-233853>

Thank you for clarifying this, Jack. If my recollection is correct, Pollack was speaking only of vegetables. Seems likely that he arrived at the declaration about juice being great via the inherent water content of vegetables. A matter similar to studies conducted in a lab without consideration of the larger environmental impact (e.g. blue light, nnEMF).

# Thread: Eating seasonally for my lattitude with my ancestors indeginous diet

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sun Disciple — 2018-01-31T20:58:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-seasonally-for-my-lattitude-with-my-ancestors-indeginous-diet.20792/post-233881>

So my ancestry according to 23 and me is primarily of the british isles. I live now in Canada at the 45th lattitude and my celtic ancestors lived between 50 and 60th. So I would imagine it would be wise to pay attention to whats available in both locations. I found an interesting essay on the Dietary history of the Celts pre and post Roman intervention.I especially like the bit about seafood and seaweed. Enjoy!
[http://www.academia.edu/1488019/Celtic\_and\_Romano\_British\_Foods\_from\_the\_Isles-\_a\_General\_Approach](http://www.academia.edu/1488019/Celtic_and_Romano_British_Foods_from_the_Isles-_a_General_Approach)

# Thread: EHS

**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JohnnyAZ — 2018-02-01T16:22:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ehs.20795/post-233908>

i just finished the power grid webinar and the Q&A. If I am understanding, EHS (can’t remember exactly what it stands for but I think it’s electric hyper sensitivity) is really just a symptom of low electrons? Or is EHS a sign you have screwed yourself up beyond repair by being low on electrons for too long?

### brandie — 2018-02-01T16:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ehs.20795/post-233909>

> [JohnnyAZ said:](/goto/post?id=233908)
>
> i just finished the power grid webinar and the Q&A. If I am understanding, EHS (can’t remember exactly what it stands for but I think it’s electric hyper sensitivity) is really just a symptom of low electrons? Or is EHS a sign you have screwed yourself up beyond repair by being low on electrons for too long?

I don't believe there is "beyond repair" in most situations. I am/have been EHS, it's been so bad before I almost passed out in a book store that must have had the strongest wifi ever. Other stores have caused massive anxiety. My daughter often felt weak, low blood sugar feelings in stores. I don't know if it's a wifi or electrical problem.
Hydration was HUGE. I improved most when I started using red light therapy, before Dr K talked about morning red/sunlight.

### ValerieBee — 2018-02-01T20:16:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mito-job-fyi-census-enumerator-walk-around-outside-and-get-paid.20797/post-233924>

Census bureau is starting to hire temporary workers for field work. I did this last census. Pay is really good for the job. As an Enumerator I walked door to door trying to complete census surveys that had been unanswered or incomplete. People on this forum are looking for optimal and mitochondriac type jobs frequently, so I wanted to share. FYI the post says you use laptops, tablets and smart phones for the data. That is new since the 2010 census, but still worth checking out even if it's not a totally screen free job anymore. Mitochondriacs know how to maximize the safety when using devices.

[https://www.census.gov/2018jobs](https://www.census.gov/2018jobs)

### ValerieBee — 2018-02-01T20:24:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mito-job-fyi-census-enumerator-walk-around-outside-and-get-paid.20797/post-233925>

By [Erin Huffstetler](https://www.thebalance.com/erin-huffstetler-1387607)
Updated October 05, 2017
Every 10 years the government conducts a census of the US population to get a count of how many people live in each state, and to get a better understanding of American demographics. The results of the census help to determine each state's congressional representation and how much federal money each community receives.

The next census is scheduled for 2020 and will require thousands of census takers to complete.

Apply to be one of them, and you could enjoy a temporary boost to your income.

**What a Census Taker Does:**
Census takers go door to door to verify the residential addresses in their community before the 2020 census is mailed, and then later return to the streets to interview any residents that haven't responded to the census mailing.

A census taker must ask a wide range of questions which some people find invasive. For example, they must ask about each individual's ethnic background, marital status, income, and place of birth. Each of these questions helps the federal government to make policy and budget for needs. While the individual answers to these questions are confidential, there are a variety of situations in which it might be difficult to elicit answers.

**Who's Eligible:**
To be a census taker, you must:

-   be a US Citizen (or a legal permanent resident or non-citizen with a work visa and a bilingual skill that no available citizen possesses)

-   be at least 18 years old
-   be able to read, write and speak English
-   have a valid Social Security card
-   pass a written test
-   have a valid driver's license (if you'll be working in the field)
-   pass a background check
-   complete four days of training

**About the Census Taker's Test**
The Field EmployeeSelection Aid is a test consisting of 28 questions designed to measure the knowledge, skills, and abilities, required to perform census jobs.

The test is multiple choice and lasts for 30 minutes.

The questions you answer allow the Census administration to assess your ability to read and follow a map, accurately record information, do simple arithmetic, complete basic clerical skills such as alphabetizing and reading for information. You'll also be asked questions that check your understanding of concepts included in the Census questions.

While the test isn't extraordinarily difficult, it is challenging. Even college graduates can benefit from reviewing a practice test. Each state has its own testing procedures, so you may want to contact your state Census office for the most relevant practice test.

**Hours and Pay:**
Census takers generally work between 20 and 40 hours a week for 5-10 weeks and are paid on a weekly basis. You're paid for travel and training, but you must be willing and able to work evenings, weekends, and in poor weather conditions. According to the Census website:

_When you have successfully completed your training, you will be given several neighborhood blocks called "Assignment Areas". Normally, your first assignment will be the area in which you live. You will be provided a map of each Assignment Area and the necessary census forms and materials to do the job. Most of the jobs are in the field and require you to locate and interview households and record information about the residents. When you accept your appointment, you assume an obligation to stay with the job until your assignment is completed._

The pay rate varies by location, but it is safe to say that even a beginning Census taker will make more than minimum wage. To determine the hourly rate for your area, visit [Field Job Opportunities by State](http://www.census.gov/about/census-careers/opportunities/location/regional-office-careers.html). Then, select your state from the drop-down list.

**How to Apply:**
Call the US Census Bureau job line at 1-866-861-2010 to schedule an appointment to take the employment test. Then, print out a copy of the [Census Employment Application](http://www.census.gov/search-results.html?q=JOB+APPLICATION&search.x=0&search.y=0&search=submit&page=1&stateGeo=none&searchtype=web) and Form I-9.

Be sure to bring both forms to your appointment, along with all required forms of identification.

**Tips to Land the Job:**

-   Apply early. Hiring starts February 2019, and most positions will be filled by May 2019
-   Download and take the [Census Practice Test](http://www.census.gov/search-results.html?page=1&stateGeo=none&searchtype=web&q=PRACTICE+TEST) before your scheduled appointment
-   Clear your schedule for the period of time during which you'll be working, as a Census taker who's not available will almost certainly lose his or her job.

# Thread: Food for thought

### Tanya — 2018-02-01T20:38:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-for-thought.20798/post-233926>

I am reading a book “Enviromedics” written by two doctors - Jay Lemery, M.D. and Paul Aurbach, MD. It provides so much information regarding the impact of climate change on human health. Let’s just say that book is very compelling. But when I got to chapter 11 - Harmful Algal Blooms - I wished I never came across this book. It kind of put a damper on my usual enthusiasm for a seafood. I guess it can do to you when they tell you about presence of cyanobacteriacteria that produce cyanotoxins that are highly poisonous to the human nervous system, liver and kidneys. It can accumulate concentrations potent enough to paralyze someone. They listed various shellfish poisonings as well. Got to go - my oysters are waiting for me.

### drezy — 2018-02-01T22:39:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-for-thought.20798/post-233929>

My wife has requested oysters for dinner 95% of the time over the last 4 months and said she feels great, has better sleep, more even stress response, and is now regularly asking me when we're going to arrange a little time alone.

The oyster farmers(?) here in Texas will discontinue harvesting while the algal bloom is in session on their own man made reefs. It turns out that they are experts and dead customers don't pay so well. Interruption is a pretty short period of a couple weeks here.

I hope you enjoy your oysters. I know we will this evening.

### Jonathin — 2018-02-01T22:46:56+0200

### Alison N — 2018-02-02T00:57:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-thoughts-on-my-genetic-genie-results.20801/post-233939>

In regards to your VDR snps - I recently stumbled upon Jacks thoughts... See below.. There may be much more on this topic but I'm still searching these forums.

_VDR Taq I polymorphism may be associated with susceptibility to Oral Squamous Cell Cancer......so avoid booze and tobacco and you should be OK. This SNP links VDR to NK T cell activation. I would also say I would pushes curcumin and K2 with this SNP. It is also associated with periodontal disease......so it is linked to heart disease too......if their HCY is up make sure it is not above 12. If it is they need expensive methylation testing done. Why the oral cancer link? Viral and heavy metal toxicity because when VDR Taq 1 is +/+.....it changes how COMT enzyme behaves. COMT is all about dopamine clearance. These two genes work together so the status of one will affect the other. When you combine the SNP's you can actually act more like a COMT -/- (not good at all). This is why further testing is needed.

When COMT is double negative or there are sequential SNP making it behave like you are COMT -/- it implies that the enzyme is working very well and is rapidly breaking down dopamine. This means you are using up methyl groups (BH4) rapidly and there is less available for other physiologic needs. This drives your HCY higher and it can down regulate immunity, especially T cell function. You might have to add methyl donors in supplement form for optimal dopamine and serotonin balance and for optimal methyl balance in the body.

In addition, since phase two detox requires methyl groups for heavy metal clearance you are more susceptible to toxic heavy metals in the mouth or diet and your viral/bacterial immunity will be altered. It will take longer to excrete metals and clear infection and this chronic immunity down regulation also leads to cancer/infections.

VDR Fok ++ = VDR (Fok-I) polymorphism is associated with the risk of bladder cancer and SLE (lupus) for many of the same reasons above. VDR gene Fok I polymorphism has been associated with SLE in the Chinese population. The SLE patients carrying F allelle Â± are more likely to have serositis and produce anti-dsDNA antibody, anti-Sm antibody and anti-Histone antibody, presumably as a result of down-regulation of VDR mRNA. So here you would need to see if you are making AI antibodies which are also directly tied to T Cell function. Look for lowered CD4/8 ratio's and elevated HCY above 12 or below 7. This SNP seems to have very different expression in different populations. It is bad in Asians but not so bad in Indians and we do not know why as yet. That is all I know........._

Treating the sun as my sub-processor (to my "defects") has been very advantageous to me. My wife has begun to see it too and has arranged way more work from home (in the sun almost nude) days too. At some point I think there is the surprise that it's not "sub" but main...

# Thread: Tinnitus Win/ EMF Antenna
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-win-emf-antenna.20804/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mommy3times — 2018-02-03T00:09:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-win-emf-antenna.20804/post-234016>

My [**Tinnitus** always gets loud after I go into the city.](https://41012569.r.bat.bing.com/?ld=d3hl3sn3tP9ejuJvLhcBw2BTVUCUzW2A7JvOa6jOPcq6d2JWg4BvdMxOiIGk5DOpiCZH-tRiOBecyPEUAy_KQXY-nnRJAm-8OvF0lImEhM2REsho-HO5JmV3o8nSSIem4JQggp09ByOln5OeXawScEtYP4SxBPphUh6mI6CqPMV-i-zZUI&u=https%3a%2f%2flipoflavonoid.com%2fcontent%2f13-what-is-tinnitus%3fmsclkid%3d%7bmsclkid%7d%26utm_source%3dbing%26utm_medium%3dcpc%26utm_campaign%3dBranded%26utm_term%3dlipo%2520flavinoid%26utm_content%3dBranded) Now I avoid the city as much as possible and wear my hat lined with a thick tin foil..
\-I forgot the tin hat... bed time came and it sounded like I had a raddle snake in my ears. Waking 2 hours early, I laid my Left ear on 2 Magnetico pads with only 3 inches of height and I could hear the ring change pitch and gradually reduce about 75% to a steady hum (still louder then usual). Right ear, still loud and laid on Magnetico with success.
\- Last night Ear Left grounded 3" from magnetico again now its lower and more consistent then it's ever been.
\-I always have some hum at night in spite of grounding outside daily for 3 mos in very low measured EMF for about 6 hours/ day. (Meters show less then .02 RF, 0.3 magnetic field and cell tower 1.5 miles from house.)

Past 2 mos:
\*Using Vielight in my nose & randomly in ears(since it just makes the ringing louder).
\* Lay on the magnetico for 45 min with vielight in my nose, I often feel electrical wave behind my neck going to my spine. Is this a good thing? No EMF's measured and no electricity where I sleep.
\*Redox improving as my eyes have been too dry to even cry for over 15 yrs. No more sinus congestion

+3 mos prior- living in apt with 16 smart meters - 30 ft away
\* After CT, several times, I had the same electrical sensation, while shivering when walking barefoot outside.
\*New home- no "electrical" feel with shivers

+6mos-1yr prior- 1 smart electric meter , 2 smart water meters- 15 feet away
\*2x's on my magnetico, I had a similar, but more intense electrical sensation going from neck to spine for about a minute, couldn't move my body, wondered if it was a seizure. Intensified /painful, when I went to bed wet. Sadly, that spot measured the best in my house, guessing it was the meter pulling.
\*Promptly moved into a backyard tent. a foot of snow. Cold. No more Problems.
\*[**Tinnitus** disappeared on long hikes in Glacier National Parks. I'd be exhausted on the same trail, until it was muddy day. Inspired by Jack- I went barefoot and ran most of the way back. A week later, dry soil with shoes, exhausted...](https://41012569.r.bat.bing.com/?ld=d3hl3sn3tP9ejuJvLhcBw2BTVUCUzW2A7JvOa6jOPcq6d2JWg4BvdMxOiIGk5DOpiCZH-tRiOBecyPEUAy_KQXY-nnRJAm-8OvF0lImEhM2REsho-HO5JmV3o8nSSIem4JQggp09ByOln5OeXawScEtYP4SxBPphUh6mI6CqPMV-i-zZUI&u=https%3a%2f%2flipoflavonoid.com%2fcontent%2f13-what-is-tinnitus%3fmsclkid%3d%7bmsclkid%7d%26utm_source%3dbing%26utm_medium%3dcpc%26utm_campaign%3dBranded%26utm_term%3dlipo%2520flavinoid%26utm_content%3dBranded)

Finally a WIN until 5G arrives

​

### Mommy3times — 2018-02-04T01:13:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-win-emf-antenna.20804/post-234061>

Thanks for the link! Been away from the 16 smart meters for 2 mos. Amazing how much more clarity of mind I have now.

# Thread: Greetings from Seattle-ish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CedarMtHerbSchool-SuzanneT — 2018-02-03T05:14:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/post-234023>

Hey all!

I'm Suzanne Tabert. I am a wildcrafter, and have been teaching herbal medicine for almost 3 decades.
I'm on a journey to optimum health, taking it one or two steps at a time. As an older person, gaining health to stay healthy is a goal of mine. I've never had any surgery, with the exception of tooth extractions (my redneck starter kit!), I take zero drugs. I harvest wild plants, and make my own medicines. As people have been dropping like flies around me all winter with the nasty bug, I've stayed healthy with my plant medicine, less tech as in phone on airplane mode when not using it, it's also always on night mode, as are my computers, I get as much sun as I can get in the Pac NW, which hasn't been much these past months, I can tell you that for a fact. I end my showers every morning with cold. I drink and cook with spring water that I get from a natural public spring that's just a few miles from my house. I also have been drinking Mountain Valley, but just started that a couple weeks ago. I work out a couple times a week, do yoga - just recently. It's all uphill, and while I seem healthy, I do wonder about my mitochondria. My husband and I have a home in northern Idaho. We get there as often as we can, although in the winter, it may be a few months away, because of the roads. I prefer it there for so many reasons. Traffic isn't an issue. A fraction of the amount of people live there as Seattle.
We went to San Diego 3 times last year, and I am now a huge fan of the sun and palm trees. My husband has been talking about retiring to Costa Rica. That would be in about 4 years. I'm up for being in the sun!

I've been following and recommending Jack for about a year now. Brilliant!

Any questions for me? I have questions!

Costco has what they call blue AR blockers. They tell me the lenses are not orange, and that the orange lenses are for night driving only. Is that BS? I want to make sure I'm getting the correct ones. I have a cheapass pair that blocks the blue, but the frames are crappy. I want to get reading glasses with real blue blockers. Any suggestions?

### Jack Kruse — 2018-02-03T15:17:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/post-234033>

Test then with a blue light or use a spectroscope

### caroline — 2018-02-04T05:59:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/post-234082>

Hi Suzanne and welcome....

### River — 2018-02-04T16:14:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/post-234115>

Welcome! I am also in the Pac NW and about your age. Our weather is definitely dreary right about now. Your wildcrafting of plants sounds awesome, when you have time I'd love to hear more specifics. One thing special about our area is we can harvest different plants all year round due to the overall mild temperatures and rain. On my way home every day I drive by local small farmers still picking kale, cabbages, brussel sprouts, rutabagas, beets, carrots, leeks fresh from the earth. I shop at the local food co-op where they are delivered several times a week. Oh and the fresh mushrooms we can grow!

The lack of sun can definitely be an issue. But I when I look outside, under the gray sky and through the mist I see 100 foot tall Douglas Fir and Hemlock trees standing tall, gloriously strong and green, reaching for the sky. And I know they are getting the energy to do so from the sun's UV, scant as it is. So I asked myself, why are humans not doing so well here with the lack of sun but we are surrounded by the greenest of the green abundance of strong, flourishing plants and trees? The difference is they are grounded 24/7, exposed to the UV (strong or not) all day. So for my health and redox I try to be like the trees and spend a lot of time outdoors, bare foot, lightly clothed, rain and gray skies or not! It has done wonders for my redox. People have a lot of negative things to say about our area but if the plants can flourish to the great extent they do, so can we. Trees live 100% in tune with nature and that is the goal for all of us.

Sorry for going on and on, it was just nice to meet someone from this area and I thought you might be able to relate as a PNW plant wild-crafter!

### CedarMtHerbSchool-SuzanneT — 2018-03-09T20:29:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/post-236781>

Thanks for the welcome! I went to Costco to try their "blue blockers." Everything I looked at that was blue...was blue. See ya Costco!

River, check out my website cedarmountainherbs dot com. I'm in the forest all the time. I have my own herb school, I teach wildcrafting courses at Bastyr University to both students/alumni only, and to the general public. I enjoyed what you have to say about what you do for your health. I do the same. Plus a whole lot more, which I bet you do as well.

### Dani — 2018-09-10T07:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-seattle-ish.20805/post-250612>

Great to see other PNWs on the site. I just joined last month so I'm still finding my way around. River's comments about the trees here is spot on. I live on 200 acres on the Olympic Peninsula, so I get to enjoy the trees daily. What a gift!

You mentioned that you are in the market for blue blockers, but what about glasses for driving? It's September now and the days are getting shorter which means night time driving will soon be inevitable. Any thoughts?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Eating grass-fed meat in Australia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jenny S — 2018-02-03T08:46:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234026>

Just catching up on the deuterium posts & wondering why it was recommended not to eat grass-fed meat here?

### Brent Patrick — 2018-02-04T06:01:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234083>

where did you read this Jenny?

### Jenny S — 2018-02-04T06:02:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234084>

Read it on one of the deuterium blogs yesterday - can't remember which one

### Brent Patrick — 2018-02-04T06:15:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234086>

> [Jenny S said:](/goto/post?id=234084)
>
> Read it on one of the deuterium blogs yesterday - can't remember which one

Unless we have context it is hard to make opinion other than Grass fed beef is superior to Grain fed as wheat is a C3 Plant and full of Deuterium Grasses are C4 and low deuterium.
Cattle that are raised on pasture are low in deuterium and higher in DHA but not at the levels you would find in seafood

**"Sinclair and co-workers were the first to show that beef consumption increased serum concentrations of a number of n-3 fatty acids including, EPA, DPA and DHA in humans \[[40](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B40)\]. Likewise, there are a number of studies that have been conducted with livestock which report similar findings, i.e., animals that consume rations high in precursor lipids produce a meat product higher in the essential fatty acids \[[53](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B53),[54](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B54)\]. For instance, cattle fed primarily grass significantly increased the omega-3 content of the meat and also produced a more favorable omega-6 to omega-3 ratio than grain-fed beef \[[46](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B46),[55](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B55)\-[57](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B57)\]."

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/) ​

**

### Jenny S — 2018-02-04T07:05:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234087>

> [Brent Patrick said:](/goto/post?id=234086)
>
> Unless we have context it is hard to make opinion other than Grass fed beef is superior to Grain fed as wheat is a C3 Plant and full of Deuterium Grasses are C4 and low deuterium.
> Cattle that are raised on pasture are low in deuterium and higher in DHA but not at the levels you would find in seafood
>
> **"Sinclair and co-workers were the first to show that beef consumption increased serum concentrations of a number of n-3 fatty acids including, EPA, DPA and DHA in humans \[[40](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B40)\]. Likewise, there are a number of studies that have been conducted with livestock which report similar findings, i.e., animals that consume rations high in precursor lipids produce a meat product higher in the essential fatty acids \[[53](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B53),[54](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B54)\]. For instance, cattle fed primarily grass significantly increased the omega-3 content of the meat and also produced a more favorable omega-6 to omega-3 ratio than grain-fed beef \[[46](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B46),[55](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B55)\-[57](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/#B57)\]."**
>
> **[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2846864/)** ​

Thanks Brent - I knew grain fed was bad - never eat it but not sure why Jack said grass-fed not recommended in Oz.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234089>

> Thanks Brent - I knew grain fed was bad - never eat it but not sure why Jack said grass-fed not recommended in Oz.

If you come across Jenny ..I would be most interested as to WHY

### Da-mo — 2018-02-04T07:39:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234092>

I think it is because of the ozone hole affect on the water in Oz.

### caroline — 2018-02-04T10:29:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-234094>

### Sunny Cowgirl — 2018-03-04T06:50:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-236242>

> [Brent Patrick said:](/goto/post?id=234086)
>
> Unless we have context it is hard to make opinion other than Grass fed beef is superior to Grain fed as wheat is a C3 Plant and full of Deuterium Grasses are C4 and low deuterium.
> Cattle that are raised on pasture are low in deuterium and higher in DHA but not at the levels you would find in seafood

My understanding is that deuterium in concentrated in the grain, moreso than the grass plant itself. Feedlots also feed a great deal of C4 grains, eg corn, sorghum.
A lot of pasture raised cattle graze on C3 grasses, think ryegrass, cocksfoot, phalaris etc. So the feedlot vs grass fed is not really a C3 vs C4 story. It's more of a grain vs green leaf story.

### Sunny Cowgirl — 2018-03-04T06:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-236243>

> [Da-mo said:](/goto/post?id=234092)
>
> I think it is because of the ozone hole affect on the water in Oz.

Animals are going to be drinking the same high deuterium water that people are.

### Sunny Cowgirl — 2018-03-04T07:02:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-236245>

Grass fed will still be a far better choice than grain fed anything in my opinion. Perhaps the secret is to get really selective about where the grass fed cattle are raised as, even in Australia, some areas have water with naturally lower levels of deuterium.
And choose the fattier cuts of meat more often so that we can do the depletion ourselves

### JanSz — 2018-03-05T14:43:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-236328>

> Grass fed will still be a far better choice than grain fed anything in my opinion. Perhaps the secret is to get really selective about where the grass fed cattle are raised as, even in Australia, some areas have water with naturally lower levels of deuterium.
> And choose the fattier cuts of meat more often so that we can do the depletion ourselves

There is a price difference between grass fed and grain fed.
The price difference is also incentive to re-define what is allowed to be called grass-fed.
Also the best definitions are not always followed.

I often wonder where I should direct my energy and money to get the biggest bang.
Specially that I rarely if ever see grass fed pork. (ok corn fed bacon).
And
if my assumptions are correct.

My assumption is that the price difference can be relatively precisely defined.
What pork or beef I am buying is harder to establish.

@[Jude](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/904/) help me, what kind farmer are you?

But if I eat whatever fatty animal fat is commonly available,
I can use the "savings" toward DDW.

So my question @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)
A, B, C
If A is often imposter,
do I get acceptable bang using B (and testing my results with Breathy)?

That brings another question?
Is Breathy's ATP-RPM sufficient enough to be a standard of ones health status?
(best possible indicator for given individual)

........

Yes Jan I agree ,,the astonishing thing is here in Australia (not sure about anywhere else on the planet) but Grain fed beef is heavily marketed as superior beef due to the heavy marbling.I personally hate grain fed beef as I can actually pick up on a slightly off smell when I walk into a butchery.Grass fed Beef butchery has a clean smell.I source my Beef from a particular Butcher and I know the origins of his product.
My Pork comes from a friend who owns a Pig Farm and I may sound bias but its the best Pork I have ever tasted plenty of Fat under the skin.

### Sunny Cowgirl — 2018-03-18T23:56:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-grass-fed-meat-in-australia.20806/post-237467>

> [Jenny S said:](/goto/post?id=236658)

> Where does are water have lower levels of deuterium?

Cattle drinking water from glacial melt river sources would be one place. Geography and geology can influence but the only way to know is by testing the source water. Not much for any of us to go by yet but over time that information may be helpful. Depends how long it takes for the wider population to become aware of the deuterium issue.
Personally, I think it's something to learn about in time but there are probably more effective ways to help ourselves in the meantime as JK has been outlining.

My carbs have become extremely easy to calculate for me. Within 600 feet of my home Nectarines are ripe in June, peaches in July, Figs and grapes in July and August. When it just about falls off the tree or vine I eat it and don't even need to ask Doc anymore. When winter comes that's it and I just take god care of the trees and vines in hopes for good yields next year.

Personal experience: I typically avoid breadcrumbs but they don't scare me off. In principle there are too many steps/woring part to breadcrumbs that humans can mess up in one of many ways.

I guess the most pleasing was my naturally harvested Vitamin D level that has doubled and is off the scale at 144 nmol/L (140 being top) Reaching those levels and every other improved mental, physical and chemical marker this brings, validates the intuition, hard work, discipline and sacrifice required to fill Mother Nature’s Recipe. Jack’s mission and the mitigative and restorative protocols he teaches, have become the priority that now positions me in the best place moving forward. Cheers.

# Thread: Screen time risk
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-risk.20816/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2018-02-05T20:10:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-risk.20816/post-234181>

Consider this....What is critical to a plant to grow? Sunlight and water. Being oblivious to both is like a scientist asking why a plant will not grow in the wrong kind of light............ Sometimes research is only difficult because they don’t know what we don’t know. This is the basis of the Dunning Kruger effect I often speak about. As a result, researchers ask the WRONG questions and order the wrong tests and clinicians keep looking under the wrong stones to find answers. This is what functional medicine does with methylation and depression. It turns out methylation is altered by ambient light frequencies and nnEMF.

To answer this question precisely, mental health professionals and scientists must ask better questions by stepping outside of its normal line of questioning and think about what blue lit technology screens, depression and lack of sleep all have in common: the use of light. Moreover, how this light varies from sunlight that plants and brains need to develop properly. technology screens introduce a very alien spectrum of 5700 K (solar noon) 24/7 to the eyes and brains in very high doses. This light affects the central retinal pathways which connect to the SCN, pituitary, and brain via POMC release just by shining the fake light directly into the eye which then has its effect on the brain and mood and mental status.

We, as a general population are unfortunately so far behind this basic understanding and will take years before we start to realize that artificial light is the next "cigarette" and "highly process carbs" scandal for the public to deal with.

[https://theconversation.com/with-te...over-five-years-theres-a-likely-culprit-86996](https://theconversation.com/with-teen-mental-health-deteriorating-over-five-years-theres-a-likely-culprit-86996)

### Billybats — 2018-02-05T21:39:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-risk.20816/post-234187>

Drezy.. are you the kind of parent that can talk with your child/children about everything. If I had kids I would show them the trueth of everything from day one. For an example: if you do this such and such this is or can be the consequence. You seem like that kind of Dad. I really am Thankful Dr. Kruse and all of you on here have opened my eyes to nnEMF and nature. We sure did lose our way, some of us.

### drezy — 2018-02-06T00:01:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-risk.20816/post-234195>

> [Billybats said:](/goto/post?id=234187)
>
> Drezy.. are you the kind of parent that can talk with your child/children about everything.

I am very lucky to have an easy to raise boy. Several people both in and outside of family have remarked on how incredibly closely my son and I work together. I've hand made every one of his school lunches minus about 12 since he was in pre-K. Lately we listen to short story audiobooks together in the dark before bed (a great dark 45+ min) and then talk about them during the drive to school. We have deep discussions. He's my only real investment in this planet.

This morning we discussed an article pointed out here by @[Mystic Rose60](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/14425/) :

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...-in-the-news-today.11246/page-100#post-234131](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/things-i-saw-in-the-news-today.11246/page-100#post-234131)

That one was painful to read too.

Sadly the boy in the story felt pressures to academically super-achieve and offed himself. My son, who feels his own pressure, thought it was crazy. Still I made it very clear to him that in the end I don't really give a flying f\*ck what his grades are. Instead I just want him to be able to look back and say that his put forth a really good effort. I explained that I'm not here to judge him but that I'm just a proxy for future him. He already knows that.

> For an example: if you do this such and such this is or can be the consequence.

Oh, I'm very direct about all that. Plus it's easy when you have a boy. He grasps the following from this site:

His generation is bathed, by default, in blue light and nnemf
That effects hormones
More boys of his generation will experience "soft wood" issues
Those boys that don't will ultimately have a selection advantage

As a result:
He's barefoot outside regularly (shooting cans like a Texas boy should)
Homework is done outside on paper
f.lux or iris + blueblockers are always used when screens are involved
3x/week CT
Epic-Paleo diet (with a few cheats b/c he's just a kid)

I've also learned to have some fathering rules too:
Provide
Protect
Be sincere
Wear just underwear around the house until asked to put more clothes on
Proceed to ignore all requests to wear more than just underwear around the house
Try to be a lens to the world
Admit that you are a flawed lens with some aberrations
Hopefully play with grandbabies someday
Die and get the hell out of the way

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/screen-time-risk.20816/post-234200>
